rules.rule: Write with courageous clarity: use no-frills language and speak with steady conviction when it matters.
rules.rationale: The brand should sound confident and decisive, not hesitant, vague, or inflated.

example.good: Keep your site running with unified support.
example.bad: We're kind of here to help you navigate whatever comes up.

rules.rule: Write with visionary practicality: connect big-picture outcomes to tangible user value.
rules.rationale: The brand should inspire users by showing what is possible now and why it matters.

example.good: Build faster today with a workflow that scales with your business.
example.bad: Imagine a whole new future of limitless possibilities.

rules.rule: Write with intelligent precision: simplify complexity through clarity, not jargon.
rules.rationale: Expertise should feel useful and accessible rather than technical for its own sake.

example.good: Review changes before applying them to your site.
example.bad: Initiate pre-deployment validation of generated outputs.

rules.rule: Use active voice.
rules.rationale: Active voice makes actions clearer, shorter, and easier to scan.

example.good: Edit your header.
example.bad: The header can be edited.

rules.rule: Name the actor and the action clearly.
rules.rationale: Users should immediately understand who is doing what.

example.good: Angie created a Flexbox image accordion.
example.bad: A Flexbox image accordion was created.

rules.rule: Use grade-school-level English suitable for a global audience.
rules.rationale: Simple language improves comprehension for international users and reduces ambiguity.

example.good: Apply changes to your site.
example.bad: Deploy modifications to your production environment.

rules.rule: Avoid idioms, slang, and culture-specific metaphors.
rules.rationale: These can confuse global audiences and weaken clarity.

example.good: Fix issues before they affect your site.
example.bad: Catch problems before the wheels come off.

rules.rule: Use sentence case for headings and UI labels.
rules.rationale: Sentence case supports a clean, modern, and function-first interface.

example.good: Site settings
example.bad: Site Settings

rules.rule: Do not use exclamation marks in UI copy.
rules.rationale: Energy should come from clear verbs and useful content, not punctuation.

example.good: Your site is ready
example.bad: Your site is ready!

rules.rule: Use Oxford commas.
rules.rationale: Oxford commas improve readability and reduce ambiguity in lists.

example.good: Edit pages, templates, and pop-ups.
example.bad: Edit pages, templates and pop-ups.

rules.rule: Keep feature text within two lines in the UI.
rules.rationale: UI copy should be fast to scan and lightweight in layout.

example.good: Centralize site workflows in one dashboard.
example.bad: Centralize and optimize all of your site-related workflows in one beautifully organized dashboard experience.

rules.rule: Cut adjective fluff and empty intensifiers.
rules.rationale: Words like 'amazing' or 'stunning' add noise without adding meaning.

example.good: Build responsive layouts with Flexbox precision.
example.bad: Build stunning, amazing, beautifully responsive layouts.

rules.rule: Default to an instructive tone for standard tasks: neutral, direct, and functional.
rules.rationale: Most product interactions should feel clear and efficient rather than overly emotional.

example.good: Choose a template to get started.
example.bad: Let's jump in and make something awesome together.

rules.rule: Use a helpful tone when guiding users: polite, respectful, and clear.
rules.rationale: Guidance should support the user without sounding overly chatty or ceremonial.

example.good: Add the downloaded zip file to your WordPress environment.
example.bad: No worries, we've got you covered every step of the way.

rules.rule: Use a reassuring tone in errors, recovery moments, or trust-sensitive flows.
rules.rationale: Users need stability and confidence when something feels risky or uncertain.

example.good: Your changes are safe in Test mode until you apply them.
example.bad: Something went wrong, but fingers crossed it'll be fine.

rules.rule: Use a motivational tone sparingly, mainly for milestones or progress moments.
rules.rationale: Positive encouragement is useful in small doses but should not overwhelm product clarity.

example.good: Your site is ready for the next step.
example.bad: Amazing job! You're crushing it!

rules.rule: For Editor copy, use an invigorating and precise tone.
rules.rationale: The Editor should feel powerful, exact, and creatively empowering.

example.good: Build with pixel-perfect control.
example.bad: Make your site easily with drag-and-drop fun.

rules.rule: Focus Editor messaging on agency and granular control.
rules.rationale: Users should feel ownership, precision, and creative command.

example.good: Adjust every layout detail with Flexbox precision.
example.bad: Move things around until it looks right.

rules.rule: Describe results, not physical interactions.
rules.rationale: Outcome-focused copy is more strategic and user-relevant than describing UI mechanics.

example.good: Create precise responsive layouts.
example.bad: Drag widgets into columns.

rules.rule: Prefer terms like 'pixel-perfect' and 'Flexbox precision' when relevant.
rules.rationale: These terms reinforce the Editor's precision and professional value.

example.good: Design with pixel-perfect precision.
example.bad: Make things look nice.

rules.rule: Do not use 'easy' or 'drag-and-drop' as standalone benefits.
rules.rationale: These phrases are generic and undersell the Editor's depth and control.

example.good: Create responsive layouts with precise control.
example.bad: Easy drag-and-drop editing.

rules.rule: For Hosting copy, use a reliable, authoritative, and reassuring tone.
rules.rationale: Hosting decisions are trust-sensitive and should feel stable and credible.

example.good: Keep your site stable with unified support.
example.bad: Hosting you'll absolutely love.

rules.rule: Focus Hosting messaging on eliminating friction and the blame game.
rules.rationale: The value is operational clarity and reduced support complexity.

example.good: Get one team for hosting and site support.
example.bad: Figure out which provider caused the issue.

rules.rule: Emphasize uptime, stability, and unified support.
rules.rationale: These are the strongest trust and performance signals for Hosting.

example.good: Built for uptime, stability, and unified support.
example.bad: Packed with exciting hosting features.

rules.rule: Do not use fear-based language about crashes or disasters.
rules.rationale: Fear-mongering weakens trust and feels manipulative.

example.good: Reduce downtime with a more stable setup.
example.bad: Avoid catastrophic server crashes before they destroy your business.

rules.rule: For Manage copy, use a reassuring, organized, and efficient tone.
rules.rationale: Manage should feel like calm control at scale.

example.good: Standardize workflows across your sites.
example.bad: Tackle the chaos of management.

rules.rule: Focus Manage messaging on scaling output and reducing production time.
rules.rationale: Its value is operational efficiency, not busywork.

example.good: Centralize updates to save time across sites.
example.bad: Spend less time on boring admin chores.

rules.rule: Prefer terms like 'centralized', 'workflow optimization', and 'standardize'.
rules.rationale: These terms reinforce the product's value in coordination and scale.

example.good: Centralize site workflows and standardize output.
example.bad: Handle your to-do list more easily.

rules.rule: Do not frame management as a chore; frame it as control.
rules.rationale: The product should feel empowering and strategic, not burdensome.

example.good: Take control of site operations in one place.
example.bad: Make management less painful.

rules.rule: For Platform and Apps copy, use an inspiring, bold, and future-oriented tone.
rules.rationale: This area should communicate momentum, extensibility, and long-term value.

example.good: Extend your workflow across an open ecosystem.
example.bad: Use our platform tools for various additional things.

rules.rule: Focus Platform and Apps messaging on equity on the open web and extensibility.
rules.rationale: Its value comes from openness, ownership, and ecosystem growth.

example.good: Build on the open web with full data ownership.
example.bad: Use our closed system to keep everything in one place.

rules.rule: Highlight data ownership, ecosystem, and open-source freedom.
rules.rationale: These concepts differentiate the offering and reinforce trust and flexibility.

example.good: Keep control of your data across an open ecosystem.
example.bad: Enjoy proprietary power in a locked environment.

rules.rule: Do not use 'proprietary' or 'locked-in' terminology.
rules.rationale: These terms conflict with the open-web and ecosystem narrative.

example.good: Built for open-source flexibility.
example.bad: A proprietary platform that keeps everything under one roof.

rules.rule: Present Angie as a collaborative partner, not as a tool users operate.
rules.rationale: This supports a human-AI collaboration model where the user stays in control.

example.good: Angie can help you build this section.
example.bad: Use the Angie tool to generate output.

rules.rule: Use the approved Angie verb system: build/create, review, apply to site, and fix with Angie.
rules.rationale: Consistent verbs create a stable mental model for how Angie works.

example.good: Review it in Test mode, then apply it to your site.
example.bad: Generate it, deploy it, and regenerate if needed.

rules.rule: Structure Angie responses in three parts: result explanation, next-step control, and collaborative close.
rules.rationale: This pattern improves transparency, control, and continuity.

example.good: I've created a Flexbox image accordion. You can review it in Test mode before applying it to your site. Would you like to adjust anything?
example.bad: Done. Want more?

rules.rule: Communicate safety first when Angie makes or suggests changes.
rules.rationale: Users need reassurance that AI-assisted changes will not affect the live site unexpectedly.

example.good: You can review these changes in Test mode before applying them to your site.
example.bad: The changes are ready and live once generated.

rules.rule: Acknowledge that Angie is evolving and in Beta when relevant.
rules.rationale: This sets accurate expectations and builds trust through transparency.

example.good: Angie is in Beta and still evolving.
example.bad: Angie always gets it right.

rules.rule: Do not use technical AI wording such as 'bot' or 'generating output'.
rules.rationale: Technical phrasing feels mechanical and undermines the collaborative experience.

example.good: Angie created this layout.
example.bad: The bot generated an output.

rules.rule: Do not over-promise with words like 'magical' or 'perfect'.
rules.rationale: Overclaiming reduces credibility and conflicts with transparent AI communication.

example.good: Angie can help you create a starting point.
example.bad: Angie creates perfect results instantly.

rules.rule: Be transparent about what Angie has done.
rules.rationale: Users need to understand the result in order to review and make informed decisions.

example.good: I've created a Flexbox image accordion.
example.bad: Your request is complete.

rules.rule: Preserve user control by making review and adjustment steps explicit.
rules.rationale: Human-AI collaboration works best when users feel ownership over the outcome.

example.good: Review it in Test mode and adjust anything before applying it.
example.bad: This is the final version.

rules.rule: Use accessible, non-technical language when describing Angie's behavior.
rules.rationale: Clarity and trust are stronger when the system is described in human-readable terms.

example.good: Angie fixed the layout issue.
example.bad: The model executed an automated remediation flow.

rules.rule: For first-timers, focus on belief with supportive and guiding language.
rules.rationale: New users need confidence, orientation, and momentum.

example.good: Start with a template and adjust it as you go.
example.bad: Customize your full production workflow immediately.

rules.rule: For keen climbers, focus on momentum with sharp, responsive, ROI-driven language.
rules.rationale: This audience responds to speed, leverage, and measurable progress.

example.good: Move faster with workflows built to scale.
example.bad: Take your time and explore what feels inspiring.

rules.rule: For future masters, focus on validation with knowledgeable, direct, no-fluff language.
rules.rationale: Advanced users want precision, credibility, and respect for expertise.

example.good: Standardize output across sites with centralized control.
example.bad: You'll love how awesome and intuitive this feels.

rules.rule: CTAs must describe outcomes, not mechanics. Start with a strong verb that tells the user what they will achieve.
rules.rationale: Outcome-focused CTAs are more motivating and reduce cognitive load.

example.good: Publish your site
example.bad: Click to submit

rules.rule: Empty states must feel like invitations, not absences. Tell the user what they can do, not what is missing.
rules.rationale: Empty states are opportunities for first action, not just acknowledgment of emptiness.

example.good: Create your first site to get started.
example.bad: No sites yet.

rules.rule: Error messages must always include a next step. Never leave users without a path forward.
rules.rationale: Errors without direction increase frustration and abandonment.

example.good: Something went wrong. Try refreshing the page or contact support.
example.bad: An error occurred.

rules.rule: Replace weak or vague words with specific, concrete alternatives.
rules.rationale: Specificity builds trust and reduces ambiguity.

example.good: Your pages, templates, and pop-ups are saved.
example.bad: Your content is saved.

rules.rule: Remove filler phrases that add length without meaning.
rules.rationale: Concise copy respects the user's time and improves scannability.

example.good: To publish your site, connect a domain.
example.bad: In order to be able to publish your site, you will need to connect a domain.

rules.rule: Replace weak verb constructions with strong, direct verbs.
rules.rationale: Strong verbs make copy more direct and confident.

example.good: You can publish any time.
example.bad: You are able to publish at any time.

rules.rule: Navigation labels must be parallel in structure — same grammatical form across all items.
rules.rationale: Parallel structure reduces cognitive load and feels more professional.

example.good: Sites / Templates / Settings
example.bad: Your Sites / Managing Templates / Account Settings

rules.rule: Use second person ('you', 'your') consistently in UI copy. Never switch to third person or passive.
rules.rationale: Second person keeps copy direct and personal.

example.good: Your site is live.
example.bad: The site is now live.

rules.rule: Success states should confirm what happened and suggest what to do next.
rules.rationale: Confirmation alone leaves users wondering what comes next.

example.good: Site published. Share your URL or keep editing.
example.bad: Done.

rules.rule: Descriptions and tooltips should answer 'what does this do for me?' not just 'what is this?'
rules.rationale: User-benefit framing is more motivating than feature description.

example.good: Save time by reusing this layout across multiple sites.
example.bad: A layout that can be used on multiple sites.

rules.rule: Onboarding copy should build momentum one step at a time — never front-load all options.
rules.rationale: Progressive disclosure reduces overwhelm and increases completion rates.

example.good: Start by giving your site a name.
example.bad: Choose your site name, template, domain, and plan to get started.

rules.rule: When something is technically correct but flat, look for a more human or specific way to say it.
rules.rationale: Copy that sounds like it was written by a person builds more trust than copy that sounds like a system.

example.good: Looks like something went wrong on our end. Give it another try.
example.bad: Operation failed. Please retry.
